edges.description: PPT1 loss disrupts lysosomal nutrient sensing and activates mTORC1, suppressing autophagy and contributing to neurodegeneration.
PPT1 deficiency misroutes NPC1, leading to lysosomal cholesterol dysregulation, mTORC1 activation, autophagy inhibition, and neuronal injury in CLN1 model systems.
Excessive AKAP5 palmitoylation at Ppt1-deficient synapses sensitizes NFAT signaling and links synaptic trafficking defects to neuroinflammation.

nodes.description: PPT1 deficiency causes excess AKAP5 palmitoylation at synapses, aberrant GluA1 synaptic scaling, NFAT sensitization, and increased neuroinflammatory signaling.

evidence.snippet: the absence of depalmitoylation stifles synaptic protein trafficking and contributes to neuroinflammation via an Akap5-associated mechanism.

nodes.description: PPT1 loss disrupts lysosomal nutrient sensing and hyperactivates mTORC1 through IGF1/PI3K/Akt signaling, suppressing autophagy and contributing to neurologic injury.

evidence.snippet: Despite this defect, mTORC1 was hyperactivated via the IGF1/PI3K/Akt-signaling pathway, which suppressed autophagy contributing to neuropathology.

nodes.description: Persistent NPC1 palmitoylation and misrouting in PPT1 deficiency impair lysosomal cholesterol homeostasis, producing cholesterol-dependent mTORC1 activation, autophagy inhibition, and neuronal injury.

evidence.snippet: In Cln1-/- mice, Ppt1 deficiency misroutes NPC1-dysregulating lysosomal cholesterol homeostasis.

nodes.description: PPT1 deficiency prevents normal depalmitoylation and lysosomal clearance of S-palmitoylated proteins. This produces autofluorescent storage material in neurons and disrupts lysosomal nutrient sensing, mTORC1/autophagy control, cholesterol trafficking, synaptic protein trafficking, and inflammatory glial signaling.

evidence.snippet: PPT1 catalyzes depalmitoylation of S-palmitoylated proteins for degradation and clearance by lysosomal hydrolases.
CLN1 is characterized by the accumulation of autofluorescent lysosomal storage material (AFSM) in neurons and robust neuroinflammation.
